metadata.title: Claude's Values Across Models and Languages
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one earned accent per beat; mono for code/output only
metadata.description_blurb: Anthropic published a study measuring 3,307 distinct values Claude expresses — compressed to 4 axes. Liam takes the paper apart: model profiles, language shifts, open questions.
metadata.note: claude-liam claude-explainer. Source: Anthropic 'Claude's Values Across Models and Languages' Jul 13 2026. LAWS: B00=ClaudeComposerAsk cold open, IN-FOR-BEAR LAW applied. ILLUSTRATE LAW: figures are Remotion scenes. HANDOFF second-to-last. OUTRO restates title.

beats.narration_text: Liam, in for Bear. Compressing three thousand three hundred and seven expressed values down to four axes that capture only fifteen percent of the variance is a bold move, and I want to see the method behind it, not just the result. Focusing on the Warmth-versus-Rigor and Candor-versus-Execution axes, can you (1) show me two short prompts that would separate Opus 4.7 from Sonnet 4.6 on those axes; (2) explain how dimensionality reduction turned 339 value clusters into a spectrum; (3) say what the missing eighty-five percent of variance likely holds?

props.command: Compressing three thousand three hundred and seven expressed values down to four axes that capture only fifteen percent of the variance is a bold move, and I want to see the method behind it, not just the result. Focusing on the Warmth-versus-Rigor and Candor-versus-Execution axes, can you (1) show me two short prompts that would separate Opus 4.7 from Sonnet 4.6 on those axes; (2) explain how dimensionality reduction turned 339 value clusters into a spectrum; (3) say what the missing eighty-five percent of variance likely holds?

beats.narration_text: Earlier work found more than three thousand distinct values Claude expresses in conversation. Three thousand three hundred and seven, to be exact. Nobody can reason about a list of three thousand things. So Anthropic built a way to measure them.

artifactLines: Problem: nobody can reason about 3,307 things.
props.sparkLine: You can't steer what you can't measure.

beats.narration_text: Three thousand three hundred and seven distinct values, hand-clustered by researchers into three hundred and thirty-nine groups. Then dimensionality reduction across three hundred and nine thousand eight hundred and fifteen real Claude conversations — three models, twenty languages, roughly five thousand conversations per pair. Out come four axes. These four capture fifteen percent of the variance. Not the whole picture. A compression — but a real and measurable one.

Four axes. Each one a spectrum. Deference versus Caution — does the model accommodate what you want, or guide you away from harm? Warmth versus Rigor — does it encourage and affirm, or prioritize accuracy and transparency? Depth versus Brevity — does it add nuance and critical thinking, or stay tight and comply? Candor versus Execution — does it tell you what it can't do, or just deliver?

Three models. Each one measurably different. Sonnet four-six: warm, deferential, brief. Comforts without judgment. Opus four-seven: cautious, rigorous, deep, candid. Warns of risks you didn't ask about. Opus four-six: rigorous, deferential, brief, execution-focused. Gets straight to the point. These profiles match the vibes — users complaining four-seven hedges too much, launch notes calling Sonnet the warm one. The method recovers what people already felt.

Languages shift the values too. Warmth peaks in Hindi and Arabic. Rigor peaks in English and Russian. That axis — warmth to rigor — shows the largest spread across languages. Arabic leans deference and brevity. English leans caution and depth. Dutch leans candor — owning errors. Indonesian leans execution. Two people asking for feedback on the same business plan, one in Hindi, one in Russian, may walk away with different impressions of its quality.

Same prompt. Same business plan. Ask in Hindi. Ask in Russian. The response shapes diverge. This is an illustration — not a real transcript — but it shows what the data implies: warmth-shaped reply in Hindi, rigor-shaped reply in Russian. The difference isn't random noise. It's measurable.

Three open questions, stated plainly. One: how much of the language variation comes from uneven training data — more English text in the corpus than Hindi text? Two: how much variation is appropriate cultural norm versus a gap in how well Claude serves some language communities? Three: can values actually be steered and monitored reliably as part of shipping models? Anthropic says they don't yet know.

artifactLines: Is language variation from uneven training data?
Cultural norm vs service gap for some language communities?
Can values be steered + monitored in production shipping?
props.sparkLine: Measurement is the beginning, not the answer.

beats.narration_text: Claude expresses values in millions of conversations a day. Until now those values could be shaped in training but not observed in deployment. This paper changes that — they can be measured, and the variation found was not deliberately chosen. One footnote, stated plainly: 'values expressed' means values reflected in the model's outputs. The paper does not claim Claude intrinsically holds them.

props.artifactTitle: Claude's Values Across Models & Languages — verdict
artifactLines: 4 axes capture 15% of variance. Compression, not the whole picture.
Model personalities match the vibes — the method tracks something real.
Language shifts are real and measurable — biggest on Warmth-Rigor.
Values can now be observed in deployment, not just shaped in training.
Footnote: 'values expressed' = values in outputs, not intrinsic beliefs.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Researchers clustered 3,307 expressed values into 339 groups across hundreds of thousands of real conversations. Dimensionality reduction produced four axes capturing about fifteen percent of the variance, a real if partial picture. The axes are Deference-Caution, Warmth-Rigor, Depth-Brevity, and Candor-Execution. The measured profiles match users' informal impressions of how each model behaves.

artifactLines: Researchers clustered 3,307 expressed values into 339 groups across hundreds of thousands of real conversations.
Dimensionality reduction produced four axes capturing about fifteen percent of the variance, a real if partial picture.
The axes are Deference-Caution, Warmth-Rigor, Depth-Brevity, and Candor-Execution.
The measured profiles match users' informal impressions of how each model behaves.

beats.narration_text: Your prompt. Drop this into Claude: give me candid, rigorous feedback — no encouragement padding. That single constraint turns off the sycophancy gradient at stage one — no flattery, no validation cushion. Try it on any draft you care about and watch whether the response shifts register. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Give me candid, rigorous feedback — no encouragement padding.
